coredata.dc:title: 14 Nanoparticles for biomedical applications: An overview
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, H.A.
Sakharkar, M.K.
Nayak, A.
Kishore, U.
Khan, A.
coredata.dc:description: During the last decade, there have been enormous developments in utilizing the power of nanotechnology in various fields including biomedical sciences. The most important biomedical applications of nanoparticles (NPs) are in disease diagnosis and treatment. Functionalized NPs possess unique properties as contrast agents for dual and even triple modal imaging. The potential of these new generation NPs in targeted drug delivery has revolutionized safe and effective pharmacotherapies for complex diseases. One more step ahead, theranostic NPs are equipped with dual capabilities for disease diagnosis as well as treatment. Specifically, designed NPs have also been utilized to improve the delivery and efficiency of different vaccines, including their application in cancer immunotherapy. This chapter provides an overview of the biomedical applications of NPs and recent advancements in this area on the basis of current research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Biomedical applications
Imaging
Drug delivery
Theranostic
Nano-vaccines

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Targeted drug delivery for therapeutic applications
Application of nanoparticles in vaccination and immunotherapy

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence images of tumors containing dextran-coated luminescent porous silicon nanoparticles (D-LPSiNPs). (A) Fluorescence images of D-LPSiNPs as a function of concentration using different excitation filters. (B) Representative fluorescence images of a mouse bearing an MDA-MB-435 tumor. The mouse was imaged after intravenous injection of D-LPSiNPs (20mg/kg). Note that a strong signal from D-LPSiNPs is observed in the tumor, indicating significant passive accumulation in the tumor by the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. (C) ex vivo fluorescence images of tumor and muscle around the tumor from the mouse used in (B). (D) Fluorescence images of a tumor slice from the mouse in (B). Red and blue indicate D-LPSiNPs and cell nuclei (DAPI stain), respectively. The scale bar is 100μm.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Park et al. [36] with permission from Nature Publishing Group.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) In vivo time-dependent tumor-targeting images after intravenous injection of SLN/DOX and CET-SLN/DXO in HepG2 tumor-bearing mice and (B) representative ex vivo mean fluorescence intensity of dissected tumors and major organs at 6h postinjection. The tumor volume (C), body weight (D) and HE staining of tumor tissue (E) analysis of HepG2 tumor-bearing BALB/c nude mice after intravenous injection administration of saline, free DOX, free CET, SLN/DOX and CET/SLN/DOX, respectively. The measurement of tumor volumes and the injection of formulations were repeated every 2days for 2 weeks. Dose: 5mg/kg CET and/or DOX per mouse.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from L. Wang, J. Huang, H. Chen, et al., Exerting enhanced permeability and retention effect driven delivery by ultrafine iron oxide nanoparticles with T1-T2 switchable magnetic resonance imaging contrast, ACS Nano (2017), http://dx.doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.7b00038 with permission from Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Theranostic imaging in the orthotopic liver tumor models. (A) The MRI image before SPIO@Liposome-ICG-RGDs injection. (B)The MRI signal is obviously decreased in normal liver tissue after targeted probe injection. (C) Surgical guidance by intraoperative FMI-NIR (fluorescence molecular imaging system). (D) The implanted liver tumor tissue (blue arrow) exhibits obvious contrast in color and texture with normal liver tissues. (E) The merge image of color and fluorescence demonstrates the excellent contrast. (F) The residual tumor node (blue arrow) after the first operation. (G) The residual tumor node exhibits obvious contrast in color and texture with normal liver tissues. (H) The merged color and fluorescence image demonstrates the excellent contrast in the residual tumor node. (I) Identification of the residual tumor after the initial resection. (J) Prussian blue staining confirmation of the targeting ability of SPIO@Liposome-ICG-RGDs. (K) H&E staining confirmation of the liver tumor tissue.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Q. Chen, W. Shang, C. Zeng, et al., Theranostic imaging of liver cancer using targeted optical/MRI dual-modal probes, Oncotarget 8 (2017) 32741–32751 under Creative Commons Attribution License.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the mUCNPs-based redox-stimuli responsive drug delivery system for tumor diagnosis and synergetic chemo-phototherapy. Abbreviations: CuS, copper sulfide; DOX, doxorubicin; GSH, glutathione; HA, hyaluronic acid; MRI, magnetic resonance imaging; mUCNPs, mesoporous upconversion nanoparticles; NIR, near infrared; PAT, photoacoustic tomography; PTT, photothermal therapy; UCL, upconversion luminescence.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from X. Su, F. Zhao, Y. Wang, X. Yan, S. Jia, B. Du, CuS as a gatekeeper of mesoporous upconversion nanoparticles-based drug controlled release system for tumor-targeted multimodal imaging and synergetic chemo-thermotherapy, Nanomedicine (2017), pii: S1549-9634(17)30051-5 with permission from Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In vivo optical imaging and thermo-chemotherapy using the nanogel. (A, B) are optical imaging of healthy mice and tumor bearing mice, respectively. (C) Light responsive imaging. The nanogels were intravenously injected and 670nm laser was applied to the tumors for 30min. (D) ex vivo imaging and fluorescence intensities of tumors and normal tissues. Organs were arranged in the following order: tumor (T), kidney (K), heart (H), spleen (S), liver (Li), and lung (Lu). (E) Thermochemotherapy after treating doxorubicin and the nanogels with and without laser irradiation. (F) Body weight changes of mice after treatment with the nanogels.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from Z. Khatun, M. Nurunnabi, M. Nafiujjaman, G.R. Reeck, H.A. Khan, K.J. Cho, Y.K. Lee, A hyaluronic acid nanogel for photo-chemo theranostics of lung cancer with simultaneous light-responsive controlled release of doxorubicin, Nanoscale 7 (2015) 10680–10689 with permission from The Royal Society of Chemistry.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Therapeutic effect of MTX-DSNPs in CIA mice. (A) In vivo NIRF images of joints of the WT and CIA mice. NIRF images were obtained at 12h postinjection of FPR-675-labeled DSNPs. Scale bar, 500μm. (B) Distribution of CD31 in WT and CIA mice. CD31 (brown) expression was analyzed by immunohistochemistry. Scale bar, 100μm.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from R. Heo, D.G. You, W. Um, et al., Dextran sulfate nanoparticles as a theranostic nanomedicine for rheumatoid arthritis, Biomaterials 131 (2017) 15–26 with permission from Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence microscope images of the PLGA/PEI/pDNA polyplex coated micro needles. (A) Bright field. (B) Green =FITC labeled PEI. (C) Red =ethidium monoazide labeled pDNA. (D, E) Dissolving kinetics of the coated polyplex before (red fluorescence of EMA labled pDNA) and after 5-min insertion (no fluorescence), respectively.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from H. Seok, J.Y. Noh, D.Y. Lee, S.J. Kim, C.S. Song, Y.C. Kim, Effective humoral immune response from a H1N1 DNA vaccine delivered to the skin by microneedles coated with PLGA-based cationic nanoparticles, J. Control. Release (2017) pii: S0168-3659(17)30546-1 with permission from Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 14.8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cryosection image of injected porcine skin with a fluorescence dye-labeled polyplex coated micro needle. (A) Bright field, (B) green fluorescence (PEI-FITC), (C) red fluorescence (pDNA-EMA), and (D) merged image (co-localization of green and red fluorescence).

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from H. Seok, J.Y. Noh, D.Y. Lee, S.J. Kim, C.S. Song, Y.C. Kim, Effective humoral immune response from a H1N1 DNA vaccine delivered to the skin by microneedles coated with PLGA-based cationic nanoparticles, J. Control. Release (2017) pii: S0168-3659(17)30546-1 with permission from Elsevier.

chapter.ce:title: Nanoparticles for biomedical applications: An overview

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
University of Saskatchewan, Saskatoon, SK, Canada
Brunel University London, Uxbridge, United Kingdom
Integral Institute of Medical Sciences and Research, Lucknow, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Integral Institute of Medical Sciences and Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the last decade, there have been enormous developments in utilizing the power of nanotechnology in various fields including biomedical sciences. The most important biomedical applications of nanoparticles (NPs) are in disease diagnosis and treatment. Functionalized NPs possess unique properties as contrast agents for dual and even triple modal imaging. The potential of these new generation NPs in targeted drug delivery has revolutionized safe and effective pharmacotherapies for complex diseases. One more step ahead, theranostic NPs are equipped with dual capabilities for disease diagnosis as well as treatment. Specifically, designed NPs have also been utilized to improve the delivery and efficiency of different vaccines, including their application in cancer immunotherapy. This chapter provides an overview of the biomedical applications of NPs and recent advancements in this area on the basis of current research.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology consists of a blend of various fields including chemistry, particle engineering, biology, and medicine, with a common goal of revolutionizing traditional drug design, diagnostics, and therapeutics. Nanoparticles (NPs) range in size from 1 to 100nm and consist of various materials. Different types of nanoparticles have been formulated and are currently in use for biomedical applications. Nanospheres and nanocapsules are collectively called polymers, and they are widely used as nanoparticles due to their unique properties such as biocompatibility, ease of design and preparation, variety of structure, and biomimetic character. Liposomes and micelles are lipid-based nanoparticles with liposomes ranging from unilamellar to multilamellar vesicles, and they are made of an aqueous core and one or more concentric phospholipid bilayers [1]. The advantages of liposomes include their ability to trap both hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds, avoid decomposition of the compounds, and release the compounds to designated targets [2]. Hence due to their biocompability, biodegradability, low toxicity, and their ability to encapsulate both hydrophilic and lipophilic drugs, liposomes have been used both as agents in imaging studies and as drug delivery systems. Polymeric micelles are other lipid-based NPs which are amphiphilic copolymers with hydrophilic-hydrophobic diblock structures that form stable spheroidal structures with a hydrophobic core and hydrophilic corona in aqueous environments. They range in size from 10 to 100nm and can be designed to release entrapped drugs in response to pH, temperature, light, or ultrasound [3].

Important solid core-based NPs include magnetic NPs (iron oxide), and gold and silver NPs. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) possess important physical properties such as surface plasmon resonance and the ability to quench fluorescence [4]. Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPION) have cores made up of iron oxides that can be directed to sites of interest through external magnets, and once the external magnetic field is removed, the magnetic field is stopped. Dendrimers are a class of NPs comprised of radially symmetric molecules with a homogenous, three-dimensional globular shape and a monodisperse structure with branches of atoms called dendrons, by virtue of various chemical reactions [5]. Dendrimers can penetrate cell walls easily due to their three-dimensional structure as well as their lipophilicity, which gives them an upper hand as good drug delivery systems.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are large cylindrical molecules that have a hexagonal arrangement of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The walls of CNTs can either be a single layer or multiple layers of graphene sheets (single-walled CNTs [SWCNTs] and multiwalled CNTs [MWCNTs], respectively), and both types are capped with a hemispherical arrangement of carbon networks (called fullerenes) further wrapped by the graphene sheet. CNTs possess intrinsic spectroscopic properties such as Raman and photoluminescence that make them efficient for cell tracking and real-time monitoring of drug delivery [6]. Quantum dots are colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals that range from 2 to 50nm in size with a quantum-confinement property that enables them to emit fluorescence from visible to infrared wavelengths on excitation. They also possess excellent optical properties such as high brightness, photo-bleaching resistance, and tunable emission spectra [7]. Recent research has also shed light on carbon quantum dots, which are small carbon NPs with higher conductivity and chemical stability, lower toxicity, and stronger photoluminescence emission and optical properties than classical quantum dots [8]. Graphene oxide (GO) is increasingly used in biomedical applications because it possesses not only the unique properties of graphene, including large surface area and flexibility, but also hydrophilicity and dispersibility in aqueous solutions. Functionalized GO has been used as an ideal intracellular nanocarrier for synthetic single-stranded DNA to serve as a potential proinflammatory therapeutic tool [9]. Viral nanoparticles, especially those derived from plants and bacteria, are noninfectious, nonhazardous, biocompatible, and biodegradable, with potential application in drug delivery [10,11].

Nanoparticles possess unique properties that are being explored for biomedical and industrial applications. Important biomedical applications of NPs are in imaging, targeted drug delivery, theranostics, vaccines, and biosensors. This chapter summarizes recent research on biomedical applications of NPs.

Nanoparticles have been used in the imaging field as contrast agents, for cell labeling and tracking, labeling implants, transplants, grafts, and organs, and for image-guided interventions [12]. Nanoparticles are being researched as potential contrast agents in computed tomography (CT) [13], optical imaging [14], photoacoustic imaging [15], magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and ultrasonography [16]. Classically used and clinically approved contrast agents for CT are iodinated small molecules that have a short blood half-life. Gold has a higher X-ray absorption coefficient than iodine. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have shown unique X-ray attenuation properties which, combined with their easy surface functionalization, makes them ideal candidates to be the next generation contrast agents. For CT imaging, lipid-based structures or solid core-based (or combinations of both) have been studied. For instance, a gold high-density lipoprotein nanoparticle contrast agent (Au-HDL) was studied in the context of characterizing macrophage burden, calcification, and stenosis of atherosclerotic plaques [17]. AuNPs were supported with polyethylene glycol (PEG) chains on their surface to increase blood retention and conjugated with a cancer-specific antibody via terminal PEG chains. The so-formed AuNPs were injected into tumor-bearing mice, and the distribution of Au was examined with CT imaging [18].

Silvestri et al. [19] presented a facile approach for synthesizing water-stable AuNPs, achieving concentrated colloidal solution with high Hounsfield Units (HU). Glucosamine-functionalized AuNPs were useful in obtaining a CT contrast agent able to combine spatial resolution with metabolic information. Polyamidoamine (PAMAM)-entrapped AuNPs were synthesized with distinct sizes (≈20, 50, and 100nm) to figure out the size effect of Au-based contrast agents on CT imaging of passively targeted tissues [20]. For CT imaging of a tumor model, small-sized Au/PAMAM were more easily internalized via endocytosis in the liver, leading to more obvious enhanced contrast. Aziz et al. [21] compared the efficiency of porous AuNPs and solid AuNPs for direct CT scanning and observed that former NPs exhibited brighter contrast (45 HU) than the latter NPs (26 HU). Naha et al. [22] reported gold-silver alloy nanoparticles (GSAN) which had potent dual-energy mammography (DEM) contrast properties and improved biocompatibility. In vivo imaging of mice breast tumors showed that GSANs produced strong DEM and CT contrast and accumulated in tumors with subsequent excretion via both urine and feces. For accurate localization and precise evaluation of vulnerability of atherosclerotic plaques via dual-modal imaging, AuNPs were capped with a thin amino-PEGs cover and then conjugated with the targeting molecular Annexin V and radionuclide Tc-99m simultaneously to form single photon emission CT (SPECT)/CT imaging probe targeting apoptotic macrophages [23]. This imaging system helped to better localize and diagnose those vulnerable atherosclerotic plaques via specific targeting the apoptotic macrophages.

For MR imaging, positive (T1) and negative (T2) nanosized contrast agents have been used. Microbubbles are used in ultrasonography to diagnose liver nodules in patients at risk for hepatocellular carcinoma, to characterize kidney masses in cystic renal cell carcinoma, as a diagnostic agent in prostate cancer, to monitor bowel inflammation in Crohn disease, and to guide ablative intervention [16]. Zhao et al. [24] developed a new CNT functionalization method by coating SWCNTs with a self-polymerized polydopamine (PDA) shell, which not only makes SWCNTs highly water soluble and stable, but also provides additional functionalities such as chelation with Mn2+ to offer enhanced T1 and T2 MR contrast, as well as enabling efficient radioisotope labeling with 131I to allow radioisotope therapy (RIT). A novel biocompatible nanocomplex (ASP-SPIONs) based on cationic amylose, spermine, and ultra-small SPIONs was developed to label the mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), and the performance of this nanocomplex was tested for in vivo MRI tracking of the transplanted cells in acute ischemic stroke [25]. With the advantages of a relatively low SPIONs concentration and a short labeling period, the biocompatible complex of cationic amylose with SPIONs is highly translatable for clinical application. Wang et al. [26] have reported that sub-5nm (3.5nm core size) ultrafine iron oxide nanoparticles (uIONPs) can easily extravasate from the tumor vasculature and readily diffuse into the tumor tissue, as compared to larger-sized iron oxide nanoparticles. In vivo MR imaging revealed that uIONPs exhibited bright T1 contrast when dispersed in the tumor vasculature and peripheral area at 1h after intravenous administration, followed by emerging dark T2 contrast in the tumor after 24h. Ultra-small SPIONs-enhanced MR imaging also holds great potential for imaging macrophage-mediated inflammation in cases of human abdominal aortic aneurysm, a degenerative disease of the aorta common in older adults [27]. Wei et al. [28] investigated the effectiveness of pH-responsive SPION via a pH low-insertion peptide (pHLIP) for targeting tumor acidic microenvironments to enhance the MRI sensitivity and specificity of tumors. The findings showed that pHLIP has potential for tumor diagnosis at early and metastatic stages. Magnetoplasmonic NPs comprised of a magnetic core and gold shell exhibited superparamagnetic and magnetic properties that contributed to the concentration-dependent contrast in MR imaging. The transmigration study of the magnetoplasmonic NPs using a blood-brain barrier (BBB) model proved enhanced transmigration efficiency without disrupting the integrity of the BBB, and showed potential to be used for imaging in brain diseases and neurological disorders [29].

Multifunctional dendrimer-entrapped gold nanoparticles (Au DENPs) modified with folic acid (FA) and labeled with (99m)Tc were synthesized for targeted dual-mode SPECT/CT imaging of tumors [30]. This nanoprobe was able to be utilized for the imaging of cancer cells having FA receptor (FAR)-overexpression in vitro and the established subcutaneous tumor model in vivo. Sun et al. [31] have developed polymer-coated and fluorine-18-labeled iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) for positron emission tomography (PET)/magnetic resonance (MR) dual-modality imaging. The new PET/MR dual mode strategy can significantly reduce procedure time and radiation exposure. Xu et al. [32] have synthesized a trimodal imaging agent, composed of gold cluster and gadolinium oxide integrated nanoparticles (AuGds) that exhibit red fluorescence at 660nm for optical imaging, strong X-ray absorption (around 700 HU) for CT imaging, and a high r1 value (∼12.39/mM/s) for MR imaging. After chemical modification with folic acid, the AuGds can specifically target folate (FA) receptors on KB tumor cells and permit in vivo optical, MR, and CT imaging of xenografted tumors. Multifunctional nanocomposites based on BaGdF5 NPs and a metal phenolic network (MPN) have been engineered as novel contrast agents for potential applications in CT, MR, and luminescence trimodal imaging [33]. The BaGdF5@MPN nanocomoposites exhibited CT and T1-weighted MR imaging capacities, and the intensities of the enhanced signals of in vitro CT and MR images were proportional to the concentrations of the nanocomposites. Moreover, vivid red and green luminescence was observed by a confocal laser scanning microscope from the BaGdF5@MPN nanocomposites laden HepG2 cells under the excitation of UV (390nm) and visible light (440nm), respectively [33].

Chitosan-coated tungsten trioxide NPs showed higher X-ray attenuation than Iohexol as the commercially available iodinated contrasting agent at the same concentrations [34]. Choi et al. [35] developed PEGylated lipid NPs loaded with IR780 iodide as a contrast agent for near infrared (NIR) fluorescence imaging and modified them with cyclic RGD peptides to target cancer specific integrin avβ3. Park et al. [36] designed luminescent porous silicon nanoparticles (LPSiNPs) that can carry a drug payload and of which the intrinsic near-infrared photoluminescence enables monitoring of both accumulation and degradation in vivo. Furthermore, in contrast to most optically active nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes, gold nanoparticles and quantum dots), LPSiNPs self-destruct and are excreted in a mouse model with no evidence of toxicity. The LPSiNPs injected intravenously are observed to accumulate mainly in mononuclear phagocytic system (MPS)-related organs and are degraded into nontoxic products within a few days and removed from the body through renal clearance. These larger (100nm-scale) silicon-based biodegradable nanoparticles overcome many of the disadvantages of smaller (<5.5nm) nanocrystals [37]. Dextran-coated LPSiNPs (D-LPSiNPs), with a low-toxicity degradation pathway, were successfully applied for tumor imaging in a live mouse model. Injection of the D-LPSiNP formulation (20mg/kg) into a nude mouse, bearing an MDA-MB-435 tumor, resulted in passive accumulation of the nanomaterial in the tumor, as revealed in the near-infrared fluorescence imaging (Fig. 14.1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted drug delivery for therapeutic applications
ce:section.ce:para: To make a good drug-delivery agent, the basic strategy is that the nanoparticle should carry the antitumor drug of choice to the right target, without eliciting resistance (either cellular or noncellular), without decomposing the drug, and without targeting noncancerous cells. The drug can either be dispersed throughout the particles (nanospheres), or the drug can be confined within an aqueous or lipid-based reservoir surrounded by a polymeric protective membrane. The efficacy of the drug-laden nanoparticles is enhanced by exploiting the permeability of tumor vasculature [38]. Intravenously injected NPs are easily uptaken by passive diffusion across the leaky extra-permeable tumor vasculature, and by specific recognition when the NPs are also coated with suitable ligands (a method known as active targeting) [39]. Tumor tissues have a seriously compromised clearance via the lymphatic system, so the drug-loaded NPs are retained for a longer period in the tumor interstitium. This concept is called enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR), and it causes increased drug accumulation inside the tumor, as compared to plasma and other tissues [40]. Conventional NPs (NPs that are nonmodified with antitumor drug adsorbed on the surface) have been observed to be delivered to the mononuclear phagocytic system (MPS), which consists of the liver, spleen, lungs, and bone marrow. The macrophages of these organs rapidly clear up these NPs. This MPS trait can be exploited by NPs, as endocytosis/phagocytosis provides a platform for NPs to efficiently deliver therapeutic agents to MPS-localized tumors, such as hepatocarcinoma or hepatic metastasis from the digestive tract, myeloma, leukemia, pulmonary tumors, bronchopulmonary tumors, and gynecological cancers [41].

Liposomes were the first generation of NPs used in drug delivery systems, followed by the emergence of polymeric NPs that are loaded with compounds to mask their surfaces to pervading immune cells, preventing immune recognition and consequent elimination. NPs are engineered to target cancerous cells with enhanced specificity by targeting specific receptors on the cancer cells using complementary ligands attached to the nanoparticles' surfaces. Some important receptors that are commonly targeted are transferrin receptors [42], FA receptors [43], luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone receptors (LHRH) [44], and asialoglycoprotein (overexpressed in hepatocellular carcinoma) [45]. For instance, ferritin NPs loaded with neural drug were tested to regulate the nervous microenvironment in pancreatic cancer by actively targeting the tumor cells via transferring receptor 1 (TfR1) [46]. To further improve the radiotherapeutic efficacy of hormone-independent prostate cancer (HIPC) by silencing the androgen receptor (AR), nanoparticle AR-shRNA was formulated by aiming a folate-targeted H1 nanopolymer [47]. In this study, this nanopolymer inhibited cell growth, increased apoptosis, enhanced cell cycle arrest in vitro, suppressed tumor growth, and increased the survival rate of HIPC tumor-bearing animal models. Liang et al. [48] formulated FA-functionalized low-density lipoproteins (LDL)-carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) NPs for tumor targeting through pH-triggered release of the antitumor drug doxorubicin (DOX), which had been loaded into the LDL/CMC-FA NPs by ionic bonding and hydrophobic interactions. The competition assay proved that free FA molecules prevented the cellular uptake of these NPs through competitive binding to the FA receptors on the surface of KB cells (FA-receptor over-expressing cells), suggesting that this new pH-responsive and FA-targeted nanocarrier could be a promising, efficient drug delivery system for cancer therapy. By employing the tumor-homing property of cetuximab (CET, an EGFR inhibitor) and the drug-loading capability of silica NPs, a targeted drug delivery system (CET-SLN-DOX) was developed to achieve the co-delivery of two drugs (CET and DOX). The findings showed that CET-SLN-DOX was superior in tumor-homing and anticancer efficiency as compared to CET-free SLN/DOX and free DOX, possibly due to EGFR-mediated endocytosis and the combined anticancer effects of CET and DOX (Fig. 14.2 ) [49].

Yang et al. [50] demonstrated efficient targeting of breast cancer metastasis in an experimental murine model with nano-GO, which was conjugated to a monoclonal antibody against follicle-stimulating hormone receptor (FSHR), which is a highly selective tumor vasculature marker abundant in both primary and metastatic tumors. This FSHR-targeted, GO-based nanoplatforms can serve as a useful tool for early metastasis detection and targeted delivery of therapeutics. Harhaji et al. [51] showed the ability of different fullerene preparations to modulate TNF-induced oxidative stress and subsequent cell death, suggesting their potential value in TNF-based cancer therapy or prevention of TNF-dependent tissue damage. Hardiansyah et al. [52] loaded curcumin, a model of hydrophobic drug, into PEGylated magnetic liposomes as a drug carrier platform for its inductive magnetic heating (hyperthermia)-stimulated controlled release. In vitro curcumin release studies confirmed that the drug carriers exhibited no significant release at 37°C, whereas they exhibited rapid release at 45°C, which was further enhanced (three times higher) under high-frequency magnetic field (HFMF) exposure.

To tackle the obstacles of poor aqueous solubility and paclitaxel (PTX) side effects, α-amylase- and redox-responsive NPs were constructed by conjugation of PTX onto hydroxyethyl starch (HES) with a redox-sensitive disulfide bond to form HES-SS-PTX [53]. Due to their longer half-life (compared to the commercial PTX formulation, Taxol), HES-SS-PTX NPs tend to accumulate more in tumors, and under reductive conditions, disassemble quickly, as evidenced by their triggered collapse, burst drug-release, and enhanced cytotoxicity against 4T1 tumor cells in the presence of a reducing agent. Zhang et al. [47,54] have reported a zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) for multidrug carriers to realize the efficient co-delivery of verapamil (VER) as the P-glycoprotein inhibitor and DOX as an anticancer drug to overcome multidrug resistance (MDR). Uniform ZIF-8 NPs encapsulating DOX and VER were stabilized by methoxy poly (ethylene glycol)-folate (PEG-FA) to realize prolonged circulation and active-targeting drug delivery. The PEG-FA/(DOX+VER)@ZIF-8 showed enhanced therapeutic efficiencies with greater safety compared to direct administration of free drugs both in vitro and in vivo [54].

The natural linear polysaccharide, hyaluronic acid (HA), has an inherent ability to target CD44 receptors and internalize into tumor cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. Sargazi et al. [55] synthesized mitoxantrone (MTX)-conjugated polymeric NPs composed of PEG-HA for MTX delivery toward special tumor cells. Engineered NPs were able to specifically bind to and significantly inhibit the CD44 receptor-positive MDA-MB-231 cells, but not the CD44-negative MCF-7 cells, suggesting the application of HA-PEG-MTX NPs as an effective functional-targeted approach for treating CD44-positive cancers. Ahmad et al. [56] compared the cellular uptake and bioavailability of the anticancer drug docetaxel (DTX) in DTX-loaded chitosan-coated PLGA NPs (DTX-CS-PLGA-NPs) with DTX-PLGA-NPs as well as DTX suspension, in an in vitro model of intestinal cancer. The pharmacokinetics showed higher oral drug bioavailability from DTX-CS-PLGA-NPs (5.11-folds) and DTX-PLGA-NPs (3.29-folds), as compared to DTX-suspension.

Besides their overwhelming use in cancer treatment, NPs also offer efficient therapeutic applications in many other disorders such as neurodegenerative, inflammatory, and ocular diseases. Antiglaucoma drugs have been encapsulated into dendrimers, and in vivo studies have reported that the intraocular pressure was attenuated when the drug-nanoparticle complex was administered topically and released in a sustainable rate [57,58]. Lipophilic amino acid dendrimers have been used to deliver oligonucleotides (ODN-1) targeted to the vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF, which is responsible for neovascularization) attenuated VEGF expression in age-related macular degeneration animal models and was efficacious for 4–6 months [59]. Dendrimers have also been studied as feasible hydrogel adhesives for cornea wound repair [60]. Liposomes have long been used as a carrier for the antifungal drug amphotericin B to treat systemic fungal infection and have proven to decrease the otherwise extremely fatal effects of the drug [61]. Associating heparin with polymethacrylate NPs significantly improved the antiinflammatory efficiency of the drug in an ulcerative colitis model, due to its selective drug delivery and protection against degradation in a luminal environment [62].

For preventing orthopedic postsurgery infection, bone cement functionalized with mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) loaded with antibiotics provided the benefit of extended release of antibiotic (gentamicin) without compromising the mechanical properties of the plaster [63]. The bioavailability of the herbal drug, berberine (BBR), was enhanced (about 343%) using a new drug delivery system based on polymer-lipid hybrid NPs (PEG-lipid-PLGA NPs) loaded with BBR-soybean phosphatidylcholine complex (BBR-SPC) that enhanced the liposolubility of BBR and improved its affinity with the biodegradable polymer to increase the drug-loading capacity and its controlled/sustained release [64]. To overcome multiple barriers for oral delivery of insulin, chitosan-based multifunctional nanocarriers modified by l-valine (LV, used as a target ligand to facilitate absorption within the small intestine) and phenylboronic acid (PBA, used as a glucose-responsive unit) have been designed. After oral administration to the diabetic rats, these insulin-loaded NPs showed effective hypoglycemic effects compared to subcutaneous injection of insulin [65].

Functionalized SWCNT restored normal autophagy by reversing abnormal activation of mTOR signaling and deficits in lysosomal proteolysis, facilitating elimination of autophagic substrates in primary glial cells derived from CRND8 mice overexpressing mutant amyloid precursor protein (APP), suggesting SWCNT as a novel neuroprotective approach to Alzheimer's disease (AD) therapy [66]. Saraiva et al. [67] developed microRNA (miR-124)-loaded NPs able to efficiently deliver miR-124 into neural stem/progenitor cells and boost neuronal differentiation and maturation in vitro. The intracerebroventricular injection of miR-124 NPs increased the number of new neurons in the olfactory bulb, enhanced the migration of new neurons into striatum, and improved motor function in 6-hydroxidopamine (6-OHDA) lesioned mice, a model for Parkinson's disease (PD). In a rat model of arthritis, intraarticular treatment with water soluble fullerene (C60) reduced synovitis and alleviated bone resorption and destruction in the joints, while control ankle joints showed progression of synovitis and joint destruction with time, suggesting that C60 could be a potential therapeutic agent for inhibition of arthritis [68]. McMasters et al. [69] reported a hollow NP thermosensitive system as an excellent platform for the delivery of peptide therapeutics into highly proteolytic environments such as osteoarthritis. Drug-loaded hollow NPs were effective at delivering a therapeutically active dose of the drug to bovine cartilage explants, suppressing pro-inflammatory interleukin-6 (IL-6) expression after interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β) stimulation. A recent advent in the design and synthesis of typical NPs has also extended their application in gene therapy by protecting the genetic material until delivered to the therapeutic target [70,71].

The term “theranostics” is defined as the combined diagnostic and therapeutic capabilities of a single agent. The emergence of nanotechnology has offered an opportunity to draw diagnosis and therapy closer. Nanotechnology has enabled the development of smart theranostic platforms that can concurrently diagnose disease, start primary treatment, monitor response, and, if required, initiate secondary treatments [72]. Nanoparticle-based imaging and therapy have been investigated separately, and our understanding of them has now evolved to a point enabling the birth of NP-based theranostics, which can be defined as nanoplatforms that can co-deliver therapeutic and imaging functions [72a]. The advent of remotely triggered nanotheranostics, using different modes of activators such as photodynamic, photothermal, phototriggered chemotherapeutic release, ultrasound, electrothermal, magnetothermal, X-ray, and radiofrequency has significantly overcome the challenges for successful clinical implementation of this technology [72].

Aqueous phase-synthesized iron oxide NPs are able to be fabricated as multifunctional theranostic agents for multimode imaging and photothermal therapy of cancer [73]. Although the first-generation superparamagnetic iron oxide (SPIO) nanoparticles had diagnostic capabilities only, the new generation of SPIO nanoparticle has multifunctional characteristics for theranostic applications with great potential for image-guided cancer therapies. Polymer-coated theranostic SPIO nanoparticles have enjoyed increasing attention as a result of good biocompatibility, biodegradability, and versatile functionality endowed by polymeric matrices [74]. Wang et al. [75] developed an active-targeting, nanosized, theranostic SPIO platform for significantly increasing imaging sensitivity and energy deposition efficiency using a clinical magnetic resonance-guided focused ultrasound surgery (MRgFUS) system. The surfaces of these PEGylated SPIO NPs were decorated with anti-EGFR (epidermal growth factor receptor) monoclonal antibodies for targeted delivery to lung cancer with EGFR overexpression. Compared to nontargeting PEGylated SPIO NPs, the anti-EGFR, antibody-targeted, PEGylated SPIO NPs demonstrated better capability to target H460 tumor cells and greatly improved MRI contrast at the tumor site [75]. Chen et al. [76] reported a novel, dual modality magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and near-infrared fluorescence (NIRF) probe comprised of SPIO nanoparticles coated with liposomes, to which a tumor-targeted agent, Arg-Gly-Asp peptides (RGD), and an NIRF dye (indocyanine green, ICG) were conjugated. This novel MRI-NIRF dual modality probe, SPIO@Liposome-ICG-RGD, showed promising results for accurate tumor detection and resection in a mouse model (Fig. 14.3 ).

Zhou et al. [77] demonstrated the controllable synthesis of Gd2O3@albumin nanoparticles (GA-NPs) using growth of Gd2O3 nanocrystals within the hollow albumin as the nanoreactor, coupled with a photosensitizer (chlorin e6) for MRI-guided photo-induced therapy. The theranostic GA-NPs exhibit an in vivo size-dependent MRI capacity with enhanced imaging contrast for effective tumor localization, and also generate a potent synergistic photodynamic/photothermal therapy efficacy under irradiation due to their enhanced tumor accumulation and strong photo-induced cytotoxicity. A nanotheranostic agent was fabricated by filling perfluoropropane (C3F8) into PLGA nanoparticles, followed by the formation of a gold nanoshell on the surface and then conjugation with an anti-p53 antibody, which has high specificity with the p53 protein overexpressing in breast cancer. Ultrasound imaging experiments showed that these p53-PLGA@Au NPs were suitable for ultrasound contrast imaging [78]. Cheng et al. [79] have reported a multifunctional NP able to amplify magnetic resonance image (MRI)-guided photothermal therapy (PTT) through a surface coating of polydopamine (PDA) shell. The surface coating of PDA helped entrap large quantities of water around NPs, allowing more efficient water exchange and leading to greatly improved MR contrast signals, as compared to NPs without a PDA coating. Wang et al. [80] constructed poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) NPs and loaded them with gold nanorods (AuNRs) and docetaxel (DTX), and finally coated them with ultrathin nanofilms of manganese dioxide (MnO2). This PLGA/AuNR/DTX@MnO2 drug delivery system offers a unique potential for chemotherapy and RF hyperthermia in addition to MR imaging. Xi et al. [81] have demonstrated that Mn2+-PDA@ DOX/PLGA nanoparticles have great potential as a smart theranostic agent due to their imaging and tumor-growth-inhibition properties. These nanoparticles could destroy tumors in a mouse model directly by thermal energy deposition and could also simulate chemotherapy through thermal-responsive delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) to enhance tumor therapy, whereas coordination of Mn2+ could afford high MR imaging capability with sensitivity to temperature and pH.

Simple PEGylation made croconaine dye (CR780) molecules amphiphilic, and led to their self-assembly into well-defined and uniform nanostructures for photoacoustic (PA)/near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence imaging-guided photothermal therapy (PTT). The CR780-PEG5K not only displayed the strength of small molecules (including rapid distribution to different organs, fast renal clearance, and minimal accumulation in normal tissues), but also demonstrated the advantages of nanomaterials (including high physiological stability, multimodal theranostic ability, and high tumor accumulation and retention), with great clinical translation potential as a versatile theranostic agent [82]. Hong et al. [83] reported indocyanine green (ICG)-loaded hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (ICG@HMSNP) as an activatable theranostic platform. These NPs are nonfluorescent and nonphototoxic in the extracellular region, whereas after entering cancer cells via endocytosis, they become highly fluorescent and phototoxic with a potency of 2.75 times higher than free ICG, resulting in an enhanced phototherapy of cancer. A tumor-targeted, multifunctional, mesoporous, upconversion, nanoparticles (mUCNPs)-based controlled-release drug delivery system was developed for UCL/MRI/PAT-guided synergetic chemothermotherapy [84]. The mUCNPs served as a drug carrier exhibiting higher upconversion luminescence emission intensity, with CuS as a gatekeeper through a cleavable disulfide bond under the influence of glutathione, preventing early drug release during transportation, but improving the delivery function with the abilities of photothermal therapy and photoacoustic tomography. Grafting hyaluronic acid onto the surface of mUCNPs could interact with CD44 receptors overexpressed in tumor cells, facilitating accumulation of the drug delivery system in tumor tissues [84]. The design and functionality of this system is summarized in Fig. 14.4 .

Kang et al. [85] reported a nanocarrier platform named carbon dot (CD)-created mesoporous hollow organosilica (C-hMOS) nanoparticles that was able to deliver anticancer drugs and enable optical imaging. The C-hMOS effectively loaded doxorubicin (DOX) for cancer chemotherapy and released the drug sustainably (over 12days) and pH-dependently (pH5.0>pH7.4). The DOX-loading C-hMOS internalized cancer cells efficiently (>90%) and induced cellular apoptosis, as well as offering multicolor visualization. Collectively, these multifaceted properties may promote these novel carbon dot nanocarriers as potential candidates for delivering anticancer drugs and noninvasive imaging in cancer treatment [85]. Gaddam et al. [86] have reported that CDs (5–10nm) exhibit blue-green fluorescence with an optical absorption at about 300nm, unlike carbon nanobeads (40–50nm), which fail to exhibit fluorescence. The fluorescence behavior exhibited by CDs were utilized for heavy metal ion sensing of Pb2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+ ions in aqueous media. Interestingly, both CDs and carbon nanobeads are biocompatible to normal cell lines but cytotoxic to cancer cell lines, thus they can be utilized for both imaging and therapeutic purposes. Both CDs and carbon nanobeads demonstrated multifunctional activities (sensor, cellular imaging, and cancer therapy) in biomedical applications [86]. Incorporation of CDs with liposome opens up their application in fluorescence cell imaging studies, which is well-supported by fluorescence microscopic analysis of liposome skin penetration. These nanoliposomes do not show any cytotoxicity for MCF-7 cells, even at high concentrations; however, when loaded with drug, they can kill the cancer cells at a high rate [87]. Chen et al. [88] developed 131I-labeled, PEG-coated, reduced nanographene oxide (131I-RGO-PEG) for nuclear imaging-guided combined radiotherapy and photothermal cancer therapy. Compared with free 131I, 131I-RGO-PEG exhibits enhanced cellular uptake and thus improved radio-therapeutic efficacy against cancer cells. By utilizing combined photothermal therapy and radiotherapy, both of which were delivered by a single agent, 131I-RGO-PEG, effective elimination of tumors was achieved in animal tumor model experiments [88].

A multifunctional nanoparticle based on activatable hyaluronic acid (HA) conjugated with two near-infrared (NIR) dyes, Cy5.5 and IR825, was successfully fabricated and used as a carrier of encapsulating perfluorooctylbromide (PFOB) for imaging-guided photothermal therapy [89]. When PFOB@IR825-HA-Cy5.5 NPs were intravenously injected into mice bearing HT-29 tumors, efficient tumor accumulation was observed, whereas near-infrared laser irradiation caused effective tumor ablation. We have synthesized a nanogel using the combination of light-responsive graphene, chemo-agent doxorubicin, and pH-sensitive disulfide-bond linked HA that exerts an activity with multiple effects: thermo and chemotherapeutic, real-time noninvasive imaging, and light-glutathione-responsive controlled drug release [90]. After injection in tumor-bearing mice, the nanogel was distributed in numerous organs, including kidney, lung, spleen, and liver, as well as the tumor. There was a significant increase in fluorescence intensity in the tumors after laser irradiation, the tumors showed rapid regression, and their size was reduced about thirtyfold as compared to controls (Fig. 14.5 ).

The targeted delivery of methotrexate (MTX) to inflamed joints in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) was achieved by effective loading of MTX into dextran sulfate (DS) nanoparticles, which were selectively taken up by activated macrophages responsible for inflammation and joint destruction [91]. When systemically administrated into mice with experimental collagen-induced arthritis (CIA), the MTX-loaded DS nanoparticles effectively accumulated in inflamed joints and exhibited significant therapeutic efficacy against CIA as compared to free MTX alone (Fig. 14.6 ), suggesting the application of these NPs for RA imaging and therapy [91]. Kang et al. [92] developed a novel theranostic system, fibrin-targeted imaging and antithrombotic nanomedicine (FTIAN), which could specifically perform imaging of obstructed vessels and inhibit thrombus formation. In a mouse model of ferric chloride-induced carotid thrombosis, FTIAN specifically targeted the obstructive thrombus and significantly enhanced the fluorescence/photoacoustic signal. When loaded with antiplatelet drug tirofiban, FTIAN remarkably suppressed thrombus formation, suggesting its tremendous translational potential as nanotheranostic agent for obstructive thrombosis [92]. Zhao et al. [93] designed activatable theranostic nanoprobes against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections. This probe was based on silica NPs coated with vancomycin-modified polyelectrolyte-cypate complexes (SiO2-Cy-Van), which is activated by bacteria-responsive dissociation of polyelectrolyte from silica NPs. The investigators demonstrated that MRSA can effectively pull vancomycin-modified polyelectrolyte-cypate complexes from silica NPs and draw them onto their own surface, changing the state of cypate from off (aggregation) to on (disaggregation) and leading to in vitro MRSA-activated near-infrared fluorescence (NIRF) and photothermal elimination involving bacterial cell wall and membrane disruption [93].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of nanoparticles in vaccination and immunotherapy
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticle-based delivery systems significantly enhance the immune response to soluble antigens and therefore are considered a promising tool for development of new vaccines. Nanoparticles have the ability to enhance nasal delivery of vaccines, as compared to conventional formulations. Hydrogel nanosystems have muco-adhesive properties that maximize residence time and hence increase the period of contact with the nasal mucosa, enhancing vaccine absorption [94]. Marques Neto et al. [95] have reviewed the characteristics of metal NPs that could facilitate induction of a cellular immune response, particularly T-helper 1 and T-helper 17, and their potential functions as adjuvants for subunit vaccines. Kang et al. [96] studied the effects of AuNP-based vaccines' size on their efficiency of delivery to lymph nodes (LNs) and induction of CD8(+) T-cell responses. The results showed that the size threshold for induction of potent cellular responses and T-cell poly-functionality by AuNPs lies between 10 and 22nm, suggesting the importance of nanoparticle size as a critical parameter in designing and developing nanoparticle-based vaccines.

Rincon-Restrepo et al. [97] engineered two different nanoparticulate systems in which an antigen was either encapsulated within the core of polymersomes (PSs) or attached onto the surface of NPs, with the understanding that PSs more potently enhance CD4T cells and antibody titers, while NPs preferentially augment cytotoxic CD8T cells. The findings showed that PSs are a promising vector for the delivery of antigens for B cell vaccine development, suggesting that nanocarrier design has a large impact on the quality of the induced adaptive immune response. Polylactide-co-glycolide (PLGA, a biodegradable polyester approved for human use) NPs were used as a transmitter system to enhance AIDS vaccine efficiency and its effective transfer to immune systems. HIV-1P24-Nef peptide was used as a model vaccine together with FLiC (flagellin molecule sequence from Pseudomonas aeruginosa) as a TLR5 agonist to increase its immunogenicity. The HIV-1P24-Nef/FLiC conjugate was constructed as a nanovaccine and tested in mice. The results showed that co-utilization of TLR5 and NPs not only improved vaccine immunogenicity but also decreased the immunogenic dose of vaccine candidate required [98].

To overcome the risk of regaining pathogenicity of live-attenuated viruses after vaccination by conventional mucosal delivery, nanocarriers are anticipated to protect antigens from mucosal enzymes, facilitate antigen uptake by microfold cells, and allow copresentation of robust, safe-for-human-use, mucosal adjuvants to antigen-presenting cells [99]. Nanofiber-based mucoadhesive films, consisting of an electrospun nanofibrous reservoir layer (for antigen storage), a mucoadhesive film layer (for tight adhesion to the oral mucosa), and a protective backing layer were invented for oromucosal administration of nanocarriers used for delivery of vaccines [100]. After mucosal application, nanofibrous reservoir layers are intended to promote prolonged release of nanoparticles into the submucosal tissue. This system can mainly be used for sublingual immunization and the development of “printed vaccine technology” [100].

Among potential nonviral vectors, lipid NPs are particularly promising for mRNA vaccine delivery as they can protect mRNA against degradation, facilitate endosomal escape, be targeted to the desired cell type by surface decoration with ligands, and can also be co-delivered with adjuvants [101]. Encapsulation of allergens or DNA vaccines into nanostructures may provide several advantages compared to conventional allergen-specific immunotherapy with noncapsulated allergen extracts. These advantages include protection of protein/DNA molecule from degradation, higher local concentrations, targeted delivery to the site of action, and preventing recognition of encapsulated allergen by the immune system (especially by IgE antibodies) [102].

Covalent functionalization of carboxylated MWCNTs with recombinant dengue envelope (DENV3E) proteins was used to develop a dengue vaccine. Immunization with the so-formed MWCNT-DENV3E conjugate induced comparable IgG responses in relation to immunization with unconjugated proteins; however, inoculation of the nanoconjugate into mice generated higher titers of neutralizing antibodies [103]. Sun et al. [9] explored the ability of graphene oxide (GO) as a nanocarrier for synthetic DNA strands. Immunostimulatory CpG oligodeoxynucleotides (ODNs) were attached to Poly-l-Lysine (PLL)-functionalized, polydisperse GO, or uniform small GO (sGO) nanosheets. When delivered into murine Raw264.7 macrophages, both types of GO-CpG ODN nanoconjugates showed immune-stimulatory activity, while sGO-CpG appeared to be a more efficient stimulator, and exhibited higher cellular uptake and better biocompatibility compared to the larger GO-CpG counterpart [9]. Folding GO was found to act as a cytokine self-producer and antigen reservoir and showed a particular autophagy which efficiently promoted activation of antigen-presenting cells (APCs) and subsequent antigen cross-presentation, as well as a high level of antitumor responses in a programmable way, resulting in efficient tumor regression in vivo [104]. This work suggests the potential use of a new dimensional nanoplatform in the development of high-performance cancer vaccines.

As an alternative to classic polysaccharide/protein conjugate vaccines, novel nanotechnology-based fully synthetic carbohydrate vaccine was prepared using gold glyco-nanoparticles (GNP) bearing two synthetic carbohydrate antigens related to serotypes 19F and 14 of Streptococcus pneumoniae. A tetrasaccharide fragment of serotype 14 (Tetra-14), a trisaccharide fragment of serotype 19F (Tri-19F), a T-helper peptide, and d-glucose were loaded onto GNP and successfully tested in mice immunization [107]. An acid-resistant HP55/PLGA NP was developed as an oral delivery system to protect Helicobacter pylori recombinant antigen CCF against the complex gastrointestinal environment. Immunized mice with HP55/PLGA-CCF NPs induced high levels of urease-specific antibodies and memory T cell responses, and 43% of the mice were completely protected 1 month after H. pylori challenge [108]. Jiang et al. [109] described a natural nanocapsule (called a vault) which was engineered to contain chlamydial antigen, a polymorphic membrane protein G-1 (PmpG) peptide, to track antigen-specific cells and evaluate the vaccine's protective capacity in the absence of an added adjuvant. The results showed that PmpG-vault immunization in mice boosted antigen-specific CD4 cells with a multiple cytokine secretion pattern and reduced the number of inflammatory cells in the genital tract, making the vault vaccine platform safe and effective for chlamydial genital infection.

In order to improve current influenza vaccine for protection against multiple virus strains, self-assembling protein nanoparticles (SAPNs) displaying the two conserved influenza antigens M2e and Helix C in their native oligomerization states were developed [110]. To further improve the immunogenicity of the SAPNs, the TLR5 agonist flagellin was incorporated into the SAPNs to generate self-adjuvanted SAPNs. Upon immunization with self-adjuvanted SAPNs, mice were completely protected against a lethal challenge, suggesting the great potential of self-adjuvanted SAPN as a universal influenza vaccine [110]. The influenza vaccine modified by cerium oxide NPs (nanoceria), in contrast to a nonmodified Vaxigrip vaccine, caused notable levels of seroprotection and seroconversion against virus strains H1N1 and Yamagata [111]. Tao et al. [112] generated a nanoparticle plague vaccine that can induce balanced antibody- and cell-mediated immune responses.

Immunotherapy represents a new concept in the treatment of cancer and has shown outstanding results in patients treated with check-point inhibitors. There is a recent trend in applying nanotechnology to cancer immunotherapy toward the development of nanocarriers for delivery of cancer vaccines and chemo-immunotherapies. In particular, for delivery of cancer vaccines, multifunctional nanoparticles present many advantages such as targeted delivery to immune cells, co-delivery of therapeutic agents, reduced adverse outcomes, blocked immune checkpoint molecules, and amplification of immune activation via the use of stimuli-responsive or immunostimulatory materials [113]. Cancer nanovaccines can be envisioned as nanocarriers co-delivering antigens and adjuvants, molecules often presenting different physicochemical properties, in cancer therapy [114]. Zhu et al. [115] have presented DNA-inorganic hybrid nanovaccines (hNVs) for efficient uptake into antigen presenting cells (APCs), prolonged tumor retention, and potent immunostimulation and cancer immunotherapy. In a melanoma mouse model, two injections of hNVs significantly inhibited the tumor growth and outperformed the molecular oligonucleotide (CpG). Inhalation of self-assembling virus-like NPs from cowpea mosaic virus (CPMV) reduces established B16F10 lung melanoma and simultaneously generates potent systemic antitumor immunity against poorly immunogenic B16F10 in the skin. CPMV also exhibited clear treatment efficacy and systemic antitumor immunity in ovarian, colon, and breast tumor models in multiple anatomic locations [116].

Nanotechnology is an extremely powerful emerging technology which is expected to have a substantial impact on the biomedical field in the near future. Recent research has confirmed the enormous potential of NPs in the diagnosis and treatment of complex human diseases. Nanoparticles possess excellent properties for their use in the imaging field as contrast agents, cell labeling and tracking, labeling implants, transplants, grafts, and organs, and for image-guided interventions. They are being researched as potential contrast agents for CT, MRI, and optical imaging. Even the new generation nanomaterials are capable of dual and triple-mode imaging for the diagnosis of various diseases including cancer, cardiovascular, and neurological disorders.

Another important biomedical application of NPs is the targeted drug delivery for safe and effective pharmacotherapy. Nanoscale drug delivery systems have shown the ability to encapsulate a variety of therapeutic agents, such as small molecules, peptides, and nucleic acids. By encapsulating these molecules inside a nanocarrier, the solubility and stability of drugs can be improved, providing an opportunity to re-evaluate the therapeutic potential of drugs previously discounted because of unfavorable pharmacokinetics and toxicity. Various kinds of NPs have been developed using different polymers with or without surface modification to deliver specific drugs to particular target sties. The primary consideration while designing a drug delivery system is to control the drug concentration in the therapeutic window and reduce the adverse effects on the normal tissue. Specifically-designed drug-laden NPs can be directed to a desired target area for releasing their drug payload by a process triggered and controlled by pH, temperature, radiation, or magnetic field.

The emergence of theranostic NPs offers an opportunity to draw diagnosis and therapy closer. The low therapeutic index of conventional chemotherapy and poor prognosis of patients diagnosed with metastatic cancers are prompting clinicians to adopt newer strategies to simultaneously detect cancer lesions at an early stage and to precisely deliver anticancer drugs to tumor sites. The new generation smart theranostic platforms can concurrently diagnose disease, start primary treatment, monitor response, and, if required, initiate secondary treatments.

Although vaccines have successfully eradicated a large number of life-threatening diseases, some infectious diseases keep spreading because there is no vaccine to prevent them. One way to overcome this issue is the development of new adjuvant formulations which are able to induce the appropriate immune response without sacrificing safety. Nanoparticle-based delivery systems significantly enhance the immune response to soluble antigens and are therefore considered a promising tool for the development of new vaccines. Nanoparticles have the ability to enhance nasal delivery of vaccines, as compared to conventional formulations. Recent studies have shown the enormous potential of NPs is developing vaccines for infectious diseases such as AIDS, dengue, plague, helicobacter pylori, influenza, etc. There is a recent trend in utilizing NPs for cancer vaccines and chemoimmunotherapy.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prolongation of residence time of liposome by surface-modification with mixture of hydrophilic polymers
A new class of drug carriers: micelles of poly(oxyethylene)-poly(oxypropylene) block copolymers as microcontainers for drug targeting from blood in brain
Gold nanoparticles: preparation, properties, and applications in bionanotechnology

Assembly, engineering and applications of virus-based protein nanoparticles

Near-infrared fluorescent type II quantum dots for sentinel lymph node mapping
In vivo quantitative evaluation of the transport kinetics of gold nanocages in a lymphatic system by noninvasive photoacoustic tomography
Microbubble-enhanced US in body imaging: what role?
Atherosclerotic plaque composition: analysis with multicolor CT and targeted gold nanoparticles
X-ray computed tomography imaging of a tumor with high sensitivity using gold nanoparticles conjugated to a cancer-specific antibody via polyethylene glycol chains on their surface
Design of functionalized gold nanoparticle probes for computed tomography imaging
Construction and immunological evaluation of CpG-Au@HBc virus-like nanoparticles as a potential vaccine
Novel route synthesis of porous and solid gold nanoparticles for investigating their comparative performance as contrast agent in computed tomography scan and effect on liver and kidney function

Gold nanoparticles-based SPECT/CT imaging probe targeting for vulnerable atherosclerosis plaques

Cetuximab conjugated and doxorubicin loaded silica nanoparticles for tumor-targeting and tumor microenvironment responsive binary drug delivery of liver cancer therapy

Development of magneto-plasmonic nanoparticles for multimodal image-guided therapy to the brain
(99m)Tc-labeled multifunctional low-generation dendrimer-entrapped gold nanoparticles for targeted SPECT/CT dual-mode imaging of tumors
Robust surface coating for a fast, facile fluorine-18 labeling of iron oxide nanoparticles for PET/MR dual-modality imaging

Tumor vascular permeability and the EPR effect in macromolecular therapeutics: a review

The transferrin receptor and the targeted delivery of therapeutic agents against cancer
Targeted drug delivery via the folate receptor
Tumor-specific targeting of an anticancer drug delivery system by LHRH peptide
Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
Asialoglycoprotein receptor targeted delivery of doxorubicin nanoparticles for hepatocellular carcinoma
Targeted tumor delivery and controlled release of neuronal drugs with ferritin nanoparticles to regulate pancreatic cancer progression
Rational design of MOF nanocarrier-based co-delivery system of doxorubicin hydrochloride/verapamil hydrochloride for overcoming multidrug resistance with efficient targeted cancer therapy
Folate-functionalized assembly of low density lipoprotein/sodium carboxymethyl cellulose nanoparticles for targeted delivery
Exerting enhanced permeability and retention effect driven delivery by ultrafine iron oxide nanoparticles with T1-T2 switchable magnetic resonance imaging contrast
In vivo targeting of metastatic breast cancer via tumor vasculature-specific nano-graphene oxide

Preparation and characterization of surface-modified PLGA-polymeric nanoparticles used to target treatment of intestinal cancer
Acetazolamide encapsulated dendritic nano-architectures for effective glaucoma management in rabbits

Hybrid dendrimer hydrogel/poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) nanoparticle platform: an advanced vehicle for topical delivery of antiglaucoma drugs and a likely solution to improving compliance and adherence in glaucoma management
Inhibition of in vitro VEGF expression and choroidal neovascularization by synthetic dendrimer peptide mediated delivery of a sense oligonucleotide
Synthesis and characterization of dendron cross-linked PEG hydrogels as corneal adhesives
Liposomal amphotericin B for the treatment of systemic fungal infections in patients with cancer: a preliminary study
Nanoparticle-based delivery enhances anti-inflammatory effect of low molecular weight heparin in experimental ulcerative colitis
Mechanical properties and antibiotic release characteristics of poly(methyl methacrylate)-based bone cement formulated with mesoporous silica nanoparticles
PEG-lipid-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles loaded with berberine-phospholipid complex to facilitate the oral delivery efficiency
α-Amylase- and redox- responsive nanoparticles for tumor targeted drug delivery
Single-walled carbon nanotubes alleviate autophagic/lysosomal defects in primary glia from a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease
Traceable microRNA-124 loaded nanoparticles as a new promising therapeutic tool for Parkinson's disease
Water-soluble fullerene (C60) inhibits the development of arthritis in the rat model of arthritis
Delivery of anti-inflammatory peptides from hollow PEGylated poly(NIPAM) nanoparticles reduces inflammation in an ex vivo osteoarthritis model

Lipid nanoparticles as potential gene therapeutic delivery systems for oral administration

Next-generation superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles for cancer theranostics

Theranostic imaging of liver cancer using targeted optical/MRI dual-modal probes

Synthesis, characterization, and in vitro evaluation of targeted gold nanoshelled poly(d, l-lactide-co-glycolide) nanoparticles carrying anti p53 antibody as a theranostic agent for ultrasound contrast imaging and photothermal therapy
Polydopamine-coated manganese carbonate nanoparticles for amplified magnetic resonance imaging-guided photothermal therapy
Active targeting theranostic iron oxide nanoparticles for MRI and magnetic resonance-guided focused ultrasound ablation of lung cancer
Mn(2+)-coordinated PDA@DOX/PLGA nanoparticles as a smart theranostic agent for synergistic chemo-photothermal tumor therapy
Croconaine nanoparticles with enhanced tumor accumulation for multimodality cancer theranostics
Indocyanine green-loaded hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles as an activatable theranostic agent
CuS as a gatekeeper of mesoporous upconversion nanoparticles-based drug controlled release system for tumor-targeted multimodal imaging and synergetic chemo-thermotherapy
Optical imaging and anticancer chemotherapy through carbon dot created hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Facile synthesis of carbon dot and residual carbon nanobeads: Implications for ion sensing, medicinal and biological applications
Mater Sci Eng C Mater Biol Appl
The next generation cell-penetrating peptide and carbon dot conjugated nano-liposome for transdermal delivery of curcumin
Radionuclide 131I labeled reduced graphene oxide for nuclear imaging guided combined radio- and photothermal therapy of cancer
Activatable near infrared dye conjugated hyaluronic acid based nanoparticles as a targeted theranostic agent for enhanced fluorescence/CT/photoacoustic imaging guided photothermal therapy
A hyaluronic acid nanogel for photo-chemo theranostics of lung cancer with simultaneous light-responsive controlled release of doxorubicin
Dextran sulfate nanoparticles as a theranostic nanomedicine for rheumatoid arthritis
Fibrin-targeted and H2O2-responsive nanoparticles as a theranostics for thrombosed vessels
Bacteria-activated theranostic nanoprobes against methicillin-resistant staphylococcus aureus infection
Hydrogel nanoparticles and nanocomposites for nasal drug/vaccine delivery
Role of metallic nanoparticles in vaccinology: implications for infectious disease vaccine development
Effects of gold nanoparticle-based vaccine size on lymph node delivery and cytotoxic T-lymphocyte responses
Vaccine nanocarriers: coupling intracellular pathways and cellular biodistribution to control CD4 vs CD8T cell responses
Co-utilization of a TLR5 agonist and nano-formulation of HIV-1 vaccine candidate leads to increased vaccine immunogenicity and decreased immunogenic dose: a preliminary study
Recent developments in nanocarrier-aided mucosal vaccination
Multi-layered nanofibrous mucoadhesive films for buccal and sublingual administration of drug-delivery and vaccination nanoparticles— important step towards effective mucosal vaccines
mRNA vaccine delivery using lipid nanoparticles
Recent advances in the use of nanoparticles for allergen-specific immunotherapy
